name: Continuous Opponent-Process ODE with Allostatic Set-Point Drift
description: Coupled ordinary differential equations (Euler-integrated at each timestep) governing a fast-decaying hedonic a-process, a slow-decaying aversive b-process, and a slowly drifting allostatic reward set-point. The agent computes a motivational drive M(t) = theta(t) - A(t) and acts deterministically: eating when food is present and drive exceeds threshold, navigating toward visible food when drive is high, exploring randomly when driven but no food is visible, and staying put when drive is low. With repeated eating episodes, the opponent coupling gain gamma grows (tolerance/sensitisation), the set-point theta drifts downward (allostatic load), and the agent transitions from hedonic positive-reinforcement eating to compulsive negative-reinforcement eating.

variables.name: hedonic_a_process
variables.description: Primary hedonic pleasure response to eating palatable food (fast-rising, fast-decaying)

variables.name: opponent_b_process
variables.description: Opponent aversive counter-regulatory state (slow-rising, slow-decaying); grows stronger with repeated exposure

variables.name: net_affective_state
variables.description: Net experienced hedonic tone: A = a - b

variables.name: hedonic_set_point
variables.description: Allostatic reward baseline that drifts downward with chronic palatable food exposure

variables.name: allostatic_load
variables.description: Cumulative dysregulatory burden: L = theta_0 - theta

variables.name: motivational_drive
variables.description: Discrepancy between set-point and current affective state: M = theta - A; positive values drive food-seeking

variables.name: food_stimulus_input
variables.description: Binary signal: 1 if agent successfully ate food this step, 0 otherwise

variables.name: opponent_coupling_gain
variables.description: Coupling strength from a-process to b-process; increases with each eating episode (sensitisation)

variables.name: cumulative_eating_count
variables.description: Total number of successful eating episodes to date

parameters.name: b_process_decay_rate
parameters.source: Solomon & Corbit (1974): b-process decays slowly (beta < alpha)

parameters.name: initial_coupling_gain
parameters.source: Solomon & Corbit (1974): initial opponent recruitment strength

parameters.name: coupling_gain_growth_rate
parameters.source: Koob & Le Moal (2001): b-process strengthens with repetition

parameters.name: allostatic_loading_coefficient
parameters.source: Koob & Le Moal (2008): rate of set-point shift per unit b-process

parameters.name: set_point_recovery_rate
parameters.source: Koob & Le Moal (2008): slow, incomplete recovery of hedonic baseline

parameters.name: original_homeostatic_set_point
parameters.source: Normalised baseline per Koob & Le Moal (2001)

parameters.name: stress_amplification_factor
parameters.source: Yau & Potenza (2013): stress accelerates allostatic load accumulation

parameters.name: motivational_threshold
parameters.source: Derived from postulates P3-P4: threshold below which agent does not seek food

rules.description: A-process dynamics: hedonic pleasure rises sharply on food stimulus, decays exponentially otherwise
rules.pseudocode:
da_dt = -alpha * a + palatability * S
a = a + da_dt  # Euler step with dt=1

rules.description: B-process dynamics: opponent aversive state is driven by a-process via coupling gain gamma, decays slowly
rules.pseudocode:
db_dt = -beta * b + gamma * a
b = b + db_dt  # Euler step with dt=1

rules.description: Coupling gain sensitisation: gamma grows with each eating episode, modelling b-process strengthening
Net affective state: experienced hedonic tone is the difference of a and b processes
Allostatic set-point drift: theta drifts down proportional to b-process, with slow recovery toward theta_0
rules.pseudocode:
dtheta_dt = -lam * b + rho * (theta_0 - theta)
theta = theta + dtheta_dt  # Euler step with dt=1

rules.description: Allostatic load is the accumulated deficit of set-point from its original baseline
Motivational drive: positive when current affect is below the (possibly lowered) set-point
Eating event update: on successful eat, increment cumulative count and set stimulus flag

decision_logic.description: Deterministic threshold-based decision rule: the motivational drive M(t) is compared against M_thresh. When drive is high, the agent prioritises eating (if food at position) or navigating toward the nearest visible food. When no food is visible but drive is high, the agent explores randomly. When drive is low, the agent stays put.
pseudocode: # 1. Get current food at agent's position
food_here = any(f for f in perception['resources']['food'] if f['x'] == perception['x'] and f['y'] == perception['y'])
# 2. Compute motivational drive (already updated in internal state)
# M = theta - A (computed via rules R4, R7)
# Find nearest food by Manhattan distance
nearest = min(food_list, key=lambda f: abs(f['x'] - perception['x']) + abs(f['y'] - perception['y']))
return Action('move_right') if dx > 0 else Action('move_left')
return Action('move_down') if dy > 0 else Action('move_up')

perception_to_variables.food_sources: perception['resources']['food']  # list of {x, y, type, palatability}
perception_to_variables.palatability: food['palatability'] for the food item consumed (used as stimulus intensity p)
perception_to_variables.ate_food: last_action_result — if action was 'eat', check if food was consumed (reward > 0)

env_mapping.reward_source: eat action → ConsumeEffect with reward 1.0; palatability from food resource property [0.1, 1.0] used as stimulus intensity multiplier for the a-process

expected_behaviors.description: Hedonic tolerance: pleasure from eating the same food diminishes over repeated episodes as b-process grows
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: track net_affect (A) at moment of eating over 50 episodes → assert A at episode 50 < A at episode 1

expected_behaviors.description: Withdrawal-like negative affect: during prolonged abstinence after chronic eating, net affect drops below zero
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: feed agent for 50 steps, then remove food for 50 steps → assert A < 0 during abstinence period

expected_behaviors.description: Allostatic set-point drift: theta decreases over repeated eating episodes
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: run 200 steps with food available → assert theta < theta_0

expected_behaviors.description: Escalation of intake motivation: motivational drive M increases over long sessions as theta drifts down
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: compare average M in steps 1-50 vs steps 150-200 → assert later M is higher

expected_behaviors.description: Transition from positive to negative reinforcement: early eating occurs when A > 0 (pleasure-seeking); late eating occurs when A < 0 (aversion-relief)
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: in first 20 eat events, majority have A > 0; in last 20 eat events (after 100+ total), majority have A < 0

references: Solomon, R.L. & Corbit, J.D. (1974). An opponent-process theory of motivation: I. Temporal dynamics of affect. Psychological Review, 81(2), 119–145.
Koob, G.F. & Le Moal, M. (2001). Drug addiction, dysregulation of reward, and allostasis. Neuropsychopharmacology, 24(2), 97–129.
Koob, G.F. & Le Moal, M. (2008). Neurobiological mechanisms for opponent motivational processes in addiction. Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society B, 363(1507), 3113–3123.
Yau, Y.H.C. & Potenza, M.N. (2013). Stress and eating behaviors. Minerva Endocrinologica, 38(3), 255–267.
